ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pork tripe food product entry; no specific stir-fried pork tripe with coriander entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers cooked pig stomach/tripe as the closest defensible parent; no specific stir-fried preparation with coriander exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sauteed sliced pork tripe with coriander is approximately 250 g, comprising thinly sliced cooked pork tripe tossed with fresh coriander, chili, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, stomach, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 10924)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork stomach (tripe). Adjustments applied for stir-frying with oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish), coriander, chili, soy sauce, and sesame oil additions. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and seasoning. Values represent the composite dish per 100 g as served. No direct HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific Beijing-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried pork tripe with coriander. GI estimated at 32 based on the analogous category of cooked organ meats and offal dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Cooked meat and offal dishes are consistently classified as low-GI foods (GI < 40) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, reflecting the negligible glycemic carbohydrate content (~2.8 g per 100 g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (32 × 7.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.2, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce, as a full restaurant serving (~250 g) may contribute approximately 1300 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~32)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sauteed pork tripe with coriander is a low-GI, low-carbohydrate dish with a glycemic load of approximately 2.2 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sodium and oil content are managed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (~110 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~2.6 g per 100 g) from pork tripe
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork tripe contains moderate cholesterol and saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g) and ensure the dish is prepared with minimal added oil to reduce overall saturated fat and caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Sliced Pork Tripe with Coriander (芫爆肚丝, Yán Bào Dù Sī) is a classic Beijing-style Chinese dish originating from Qing Dynasty imperial court cuisine. The dish features thinly sliced pork tripe (pig stomach) that is first blanched to achieve a crisp-tender texture, then rapidly stir-fried over high heat with fresh coriander (cilantro), red and green chili, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, white pepper, and a finishing drizzle of sesame oil. The hallmark of this preparation is the 'bao' (爆) technique — an explosive high-heat flash-fry that seals in moisture and produces a characteristic crunch without toughening the tripe. The flavour profile is bold and aromatic: the earthy, slightly gamey richness of the tripe is balanced by the bright herbaceous freshness of coriander and the gentle heat of chili and white pepper. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~8.9 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (~2.8 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 148 kcal per 100 g. It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12 (~1.2 mcg per 100 g), zinc, selenium, and phosphorus from the organ meat. The glycemic index is low (~32), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce seasoning, which warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is dairy-free and gluten-free only if tamari is substituted for soy sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this specific Beijing-style stir-fried pork tripe dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork stomach data with adjustments for stir-frying oil, soy sauce sodium, and coriander additions. GI is estimated by analogy from low-GI cooked organ meat category in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (NDB 10924, cooked pork stomach) with stir-frying adjustments; no exact match exists in primary regional databases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pork stomach. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting minor naturally occurring trans fats in pork; this is not from industrial hydrogenation. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork stomach data and adjusted for the composite dish. 5. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked organ meat category; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish contains pork and is not halal or kosher. 7. The food_name spelling 'Corriander' in the input has been corrected to 'Coriander' in the canonical English name. 8. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning; individuals with hypertension should be counselled accordingly.
